text: Once correct docking was achieved, we began the procedure with a careful dissection of the pulmonary ligament to ease the mobility of the left lung as well as to visualize the inferior pulmonary vein.
Next, we carried out the lymphadenectomy, including critical adenopathies like stations 9, 7, 8, and 10.
Here, you can see how we used the tip of a glove to carry them out in a controlled manner.
We proceed with the dissection of the pulmonary vessels both anteriorly and posteriorly, as well as dissecting through the posterior portion of the oblique fissure.
We obtain a good visualization of the pulmonary artery posteriorly, localizing the arterial branch to S6 and the basal trunk artery.

Once we gained control of the pulmonary vein, we secured it with a vessel loop to help us guide the endostapler through the previously made path and continue to section the vessel using a vascular 45mm endosure stapler.
Taking advantage of the angle and correct visualization, we proceeded to dissect the anterior portion of the oblique fissure.
At this point, a brisk deterioration of the patient's hemodynamic state obliged the anesthesiology team to allow ventilation of the left lung, given that the oxygen saturation dropped below 40%.
This measure proved to be unsuccessful, however, for the saturation remained unstable despite pharmacological attempts to improve it.
Such complication was resolved through the insertion of a right chest tube that allowed it's drainage and for us to continue with the surgery.
Once the complete stabilization of the patient's stats were achieved, we continued with the dissection and consequent sectioning of the pulmonary arteries.
As you can see, the lungs parenchyma suffered venous congestion due to the prolonged time it took to stabilize the patient.
You can also see how two main arterial branches were sectioned, one responsible for segment 6 and the other one for the base of the inferior lobe.
For this, we used the robotic vascular stapler.
Next, we completed the dissection of the missing portion of the oblique fissure and sectioned a venous fissural branch.
This allowed for correct exposure of the bronchial tumor, so we began its resection.
The incision was made so as to spare a portion of the bronchial roof and therefore avoiding a complete resection of the main bronchus.
So,
Whilst the tumour was removed and blocked with the inferior bronchus, the anastomosis between the main and superior bronchi were made.
Keep in mind that these two were not completely sectioned, resulting in a so-called V-shaped anastomosis.
Two barb-absorbable sutures were used for the anastomosis .
A continuous suture was made, beginning superiorly to roughly halfway through the incision and another one beginning inferiorly so as to meet halfway.
Following the withdrawal of the inferior lobe, corneal stasis and aerostasis were confirmed, and two chest tubes, an anterior one and a posterior one, were placed.
A post-operative bronchoscopic exploration showed correct permeability of the bronchus around the anastomosis area.
